skippedSources.name: SixSigmaCapital Chat Notification (2nd email from this source)
skippedSources.reason: Subscriber chat thread notification — actual position list is behind the paid app wall. Substantive content covered in the trade ideas email.

items.title: 30yr bonds hit 5% (highest since 2007); median CPI posts biggest monthly jump since 2023
items.idea: CPI came in at 3.8% YoY in April — driven by gasoline but broad-based well beyond it. The median CPI is up 2.8% over the past year and just posted its largest single-month increase since 2023. The 30-year T-bond yield now carries a 5% handle, its highest level since 2007. The 10-year hasn't broken to new highs yet — but the 4-decade pattern of lower highs in yields has been replaced by a clear path of higher highs.
items.angle: 30yr at 5% is the discount rate that reprices everything simultaneously — REITs, equity multiples, long-duration assets. Median CPI is the cleanest measure of underlying inflation — strip the outliers, and it's re-accelerating. When that posts its biggest monthly jump since 2023, the 'progress on inflation' narrative is in trouble heading into a new Fed Chair.
items.tweetA: 30yr bonds: 5% handle. Highest since 2007. Median CPI just posted its biggest single-month increase since 2023. The 4-decade pattern of lower highs in yields has flipped to higher highs. The question isn't whether inflation comes down — it's what a new Fed Chair does when the prior consensus was wrong.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching headline CPI at 3.8%. I'm watching median CPI — up 2.8%, largest monthly jump since 2023. That strips out the outliers. And it's re-accelerating. The Fed has a structural inflation problem that isn't just gasoline prices.

charts.desc: 30yr bond yield chart showing 5% handle vs. historical context; trend showing higher highs replacing the 4-decade lower-highs pattern. CHART WORTH SAVING.

items.title: New Fed Chair test: FedWatch flipped cuts to hikes in one month; Dow historically -10 to -15% in first 6 months of new tenure
items.idea: CME FedWatch now shows a 50% chance the fed funds rate stays unchanged by year-end — but the remaining distribution has completely flipped in one month: mid-April it was skewed toward rate cuts; today it is skewed toward rate hikes. Kevin Warsh steps into the Chairman role at the Federal Reserve. Ned Davis Research data: the Dow has seen a median decline of 10% (and average decline of 15%) within the first six months of every new Fed Chair's tenure.
items.angle: Every new Fed Chair gets tested. Warsh inherits 5% 30yr yields, 3.8% CPI, markets at all-time highs, and a sentiment distribution that just flipped from cuts to hikes. Arguably the hardest starting position since Volcker. The historical base rate for pain in this setup is high.
items.tweetA: Every new Fed Chair gets tested. Ned Davis Research: median Dow drawdown in first 6 months is -10%. Average is -15%. Warsh inherits 5% 30yr yields, 3.8% CPI, and a market priced for Fed cooperation. The FedWatch distribution just flipped from cuts to hikes in one month. History is not being subtle here.
items.tweetB: The FedWatch distribution flipped completely in one month. Mid-April: skewed toward cuts. Today: skewed toward hikes. Kevin Warsh hasn't even sat down yet. The bond market is already writing the first chapter of his tenure — and it doesn't look friendly to the current equity multiple.

charts.desc: Historical chart of Dow performance in first 6 months of each new Fed Chair's tenure (Ned Davis Research). Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Iran deal odds collapsed from 50% to 26% in one week; Trump didn't ask Xi to intervene; July only 34%
items.idea: Polymarket prices just a 26% chance of a permanent Iran deal by end of June — down from 50% a week ago. July is only 34%. Trump explicitly stated he did not ask President Xi to intervene or broker a deal, removing the China wildcard. Both sides appear to believe they have the upper hand — a setup described as 'not conducive to a quick conclusion.'
items.angle: 50% to 26% in one week on Polymarket is not noise — that is a fundamental repricing. The removal of the Xi wildcard is the most important tell: the US isn't willing to internationalize the off-ramp. Bilateral stalemate becomes the base case. Oil geopolitics and Middle East risk assets stay elevated through summer.
items.tweetA: Iran deal odds got cut in half in one week. Was 50% by end of June on Polymarket. Now 26%. Trump explicitly said he did not ask Xi to intervene — the China off-ramp is closed. July is just 34%. If both sides think they're winning, this drags through summer. Oil geopolitics stay very relevant.
items.tweetB: Most underreported move this week: Iran deal probability fell 50% to 26% on Polymarket in 7 days. Trump denied asking Xi for help. That is not a de-escalation signal. That is a signal the US is comfortable with an extended conflict timeline — and markets have not fully digested what that means for summer positioning.

items.title: NVDA reports this week alongside 18 other SPX names — AI earnings season climax with Labor Day volume cliff right after
items.idea: Nvidia reports this week as part of a 19-SPX-component earnings week including Walmart, Home Depot, ADI, Lowe's, Intuit, TJX, and Deere. The AI sector has shown 'extraordinary strength' not just in corporate earnings but in GDP estimates. Critical timing: US Labor Day falls Monday May 25, meaning trading volumes are expected to drop considerably post-NVDA — all price discovery is compressed into one session with a volume cliff immediately following.
items.angle: NVDA guidance vs. the $1T+ in hyperscaler AI capex commitments is the key delta. Miss guidance even modestly and the entire AI infrastructure thesis gets stress-tested. Beat it and the next leg is validated. The Labor Day volume cliff after earnings means moves get amplified and bid-ask spreads widen.
items.tweetA: NVDA reports this week. One number matters: does forward guidance match the $1T+ in hyperscaler AI capex commitments? That gap between what they are spending and what NVDA says it is seeing is the only thing that moves the AI infrastructure trade. Everything else this week is noise.
items.tweetB: NVDA reports + Labor Day weekend immediately after = all price discovery compressed into one session, then volumes fall off a cliff. 19 SPX names total but everyone knows which one writes the AI narrative for Q3. Position before Thursday or you are chasing into thin markets.

items.title: Russell 2000 worst week since November (-2.4%) while S&P 500 holds 7-week streak — rate sensitivity divergence widening
items.idea: The Russell 2000 fell -2.4% last week — its worst performance since November — while the S&P 500 barely moved (+0.1%), maintaining a 7-week win streak (longest since 2023). Nasdaq -0.1%, DJIA -0.2%. Small caps carry significantly more floating-rate debt and weaker balance sheets, making them materially more exposed to rising interest rates. The divergence is widening as bond yields push higher.
items.angle: Small caps feel rate pressure first — more floating-rate debt, shorter refinancing cycles, weaker access to capital markets. The 7-week S&P streak is technically accurate but increasingly concentrated. Historically this divergence resolves one of two ways: rate expectations ease and small caps recover, or large caps catch down. The second resolution is more common when the divergence is driven by rising yields.
items.tweetA: S&P 500: +0.1%, keeping a 7-week win streak alive (longest since 2023). Russell 2000: -2.4%, worst week since November. Small caps with floating-rate debt feel rising yields first. This is not a buy-everything market. It is a very narrow market with a widening fault line between rate-sensitive and mega-cap AI.
items.tweetB: The S&P's 7-week streak is accurate. What it is hiding: Russell 2000 just had its worst week since November. -2.4% for the average small company while mega-cap AI held the index flat. That is not broad participation. That is concentration risk on top of a rising rate environment.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the breadth breakdown.

items.title: Powell steps into the background as Warsh takes helm; Waller becomes the key Fed voice; April minutes this week
items.idea: With Warsh now as Chair, Jerome Powell is explicitly stepping 'into the background.' Gov. Waller — who was a meaningful swing voter on rate decisions last year — speaks twice this week and becomes the institution's most important real-time voice. Gov. Barr described as 'fairly hawkish.' Philadelphia Fed President Paulson (2026 FOMC voter, more dovish) worth monitoring. The April Fed minutes — last collective statement before Warsh's restructuring of the institution — arrive this week.
items.angle: The institutional power structure of the Fed is quietly reshuffling in real time. Waller speaking twice in Warsh's first week as Chair sets the tone for the new regime. If his language has shifted hawkish relative to 2025, the rate path has changed and markets have not priced a personality change at the top. April minutes vs. Waller's current tone is the information delta.
items.tweetA: Powell is stepping into the background. Waller speaks twice this week. In Warsh's Fed, Waller IS the signal. He was the swing vote on cuts last year. If his language has shifted hawkish, the rate path has changed — and markets have not priced a leadership personality change at the Fed.
items.tweetB: April Fed minutes drop this week — last window into the collective Fed mind before Warsh took the chair. Combined with Waller speaking twice and Barr going hawkish: this week tells you more about the next 12 months of Fed policy direction than any single press conference will.

items.title: Japan Q1 GDP +1.3% ann. expected; China April IP +6.2%; UK CPI at 2.98%; 20yr Treasury auction expected poor
items.idea: Key global data this week: Japan Q1 GDP expected +1.3% annualized QoQ real growth (Deutsche Bank); China April industrial production +6.2% YoY, retail sales +1.9%, investment +1.8% YTD YoY; UK headline CPI expected 2.98% YoY (just under 3%), core 2.61%. Germany IFO survey Friday. US 20-year Treasury auction expected challenging given elevated bond volatility and thin liquidity for this duration.
items.angle: UK CPI at 2.98% is the knife's edge for the Bank of England — one hot month kills June cut expectations. Japan positive GDP changes BoJ rate path calculus and removes the last G7 ultra-dove. China IP at 6.2% would confirm manufacturing is holding through the trade war. These three prints simultaneously reframe global rate expectations while everyone is focused on NVDA.
items.tweetA: Japan Q1 GDP (+1.3% ann.), China April IP (+6.2%), UK CPI (~2.98%) all print this week. If all three come in near consensus, you have synchronized global growth absorbing rate pressure better than the bears expected. That reframes the H2 risk narrative while everyone is focused on NVDA.
items.tweetB: UK CPI expected at 2.98% this week — one bad print from 3%, which kills BoE June cut expectations. China IP at +6.2% despite tariffs. Japan potentially positive Q1 GDP. The international macro data this week is more consequential for global rate paths than it is getting credit for.

items.title: Not a bubble: $RSP, $SPXT, Dow, Russell all at ATH; Value Line Arithmetic Index fits in a linear regression channel
items.idea: Equal-weight S&P ($RSP) is at all-time highs. S&P 500 ex-Technology ($SPXT) is at all-time highs. Dow Jones at ATHs (+17.8% YoY). Russell 2000 at ATHs (+34% YoY) — but only +25% over the past 5 years with two -30% drawdowns in that window. The Value Line Arithmetic Index, which equally weights every U.S. stock regardless of market cap, fits perfectly in a linear regression channel from late 2022. Bull markets have stair-step consolidations. Bubbles have exponential curves.
items.angle: The 'only 7 stocks are going up' narrative is factually wrong — $SPXT (ex-tech) is at ATHs. The 'it is a bubble' narrative is mathematically wrong — Russell 2000 +25% over 5 years with two 30% drawdowns is not bubble math by any definition. The real risk is a normal cyclical consolidation that people mistake for the end because they confused a bull market with a bubble.
items.tweetA: S&P ex-tech $SPXT: ATH. Equal-weight $RSP: ATH. Dow: +17.8% YoY at ATH. Russell 2000: +34% YoY but just +25% over 5 years with two -30% drawdowns. Value Line Arithmetic Index sits in a linear regression channel. This is not a bubble. It is a bull market. Those two things have very different endings.
items.tweetB: The 'only tech is going up' narrative: false — S&P ex-tech $SPXT is at all-time highs. The 'bubble' narrative: also false — Russell 2000 +34% YoY but +25% over 5 years with two massive drawdowns. Bubbles are exponential. This is stair-step. Completely different animal, completely different playbook.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: Multiple breadth charts — $VUG, $VTV, $RSP all at ATHs in uptrends. The Value Line Arithmetic Index in a linear regression channel is the key visual — it falsifies the bubble thesis in one picture.

items.title: Two Navy EA-18G Growlers collide midair at Idaho AFB air show; all 4 crew ejected safely; cross-service investigation begins
items.idea: Two U.S. Navy E/A-18G Growler electronic warfare aircraft collided midair during the Gunfighter Skies Air Show at Mountain Home Air Force Base, Idaho. All 4 crew members ejected safely (2 per aircraft). Collision occurred roughly 2 miles off base, triggering full lockdown. Key complication: Navy jets at an Air Force installation creates cross-service jurisdiction complexity — separate safety investigation chains, competing institutional priorities, distinct reporting rules. Air show demonstration profiles are inherently higher-risk: tight geometry, scripted crossovers, low altitude, performance optimized for spectators not safety margin.
items.angle: The E/A-18G Growler is the Navy's primary electronic warfare jamming platform — essential for SEAD missions in any peer conflict. The Growler fleet is finite and irreplaceable on short notice. Even a temporary safety stand-down has Pacific-theater readiness implications. Secondary: air show waiver politics — DoD's institutional incentive to restore 'normal' conflicts directly with the safety investigator's incentive to freeze everything.
items.tweetA: Two E/A-18G Growlers — the Navy's primary EW jamming aircraft for SEAD missions in any peer conflict — just collided midair at an Idaho air show. All 4 crew ejected safely. The Growler fleet is finite. Even a temporary safety stand-down has Pacific-theater readiness implications. This is not just a news story.
items.tweetB: The Idaho midair collision structural complication: Navy Growlers at an Air Force base means two separate safety investigation chains, two PAO offices, competing institutional priorities. Time to credible facts is weeks not hours. And the DoD air show circuit — a recruiting instrument — just got a major new liability data point.

items.title: Only 1/3 of family businesses create value after CEO succession; US boomer retirement wave = unprecedented ownership transfer
items.idea: McKinsey research: only approximately one-third of companies create value after a CEO succession in family-owned businesses. These businesses account for more than 70% of global GDP and 60% of the worldwide workforce. Nonfamily successors deliver more consistent results; family successors unlock the greatest upside when transitions are handled deliberately and planned early. The US is on the brink of an unprecedented wave of small-business ownership transitions as baby boomers retire, posing structural risks to community employment and tax bases.
items.angle: This is the operational counterpart of the Great Wealth Transfer. The $84 trillion in financial assets transferring is the headline. The small business succession wave is the economic-communities story: when 2/3 of transitions fail to create value, those businesses get acquired at distressed multiples, shut down, or hollowed out. PE middle market, SBA lenders, and business brokers are the structural beneficiaries of this failure rate.
items.tweetA: Family businesses are more than 70% of global GDP and 60% of the worldwide workforce. Only 1 in 3 creates value after CEO succession. The US is entering the largest small-business ownership transfer wave in history as boomers retire. Two-thirds of those transitions will fail to create value. PE middle market and SBA lenders are positioned for this. Main Street is not.
items.tweetB: Everyone talks about the Great Wealth Transfer — $84T in financial assets. Nobody talks about the operational version: millions of boomer-owned businesses without succession plans. McKinsey: 1 in 3 creates value on CEO handoff. The other two-thirds get sold cheap, closed, or hollowed out. That is employment and tax base risk for hundreds of communities.

items.title: Last week: ASTS +16%, NOK +24%, CRWV +16%, CIFR +19%; 24 thematic setups for week of May 18th
items.idea: SixSigmaCapital watchlist performers from last week: AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) 74 to 86 (+16%), Nokia (NOK) 12.4 to 15.4 (+24%), CoreWeave (CRWV) 101 to 117 (+16%), Cipher Mining (CIFR) 18.5 to 22 (+19%), CRCL 109 to 130 (+19%). For week of May 18th, 24 thematic setups with ATR-based entry levels and stop-loss frameworks. Watchlist spans AI compute infrastructure (CoreWeave), space-based connectivity (AST SpaceMobile), crypto mining (Cipher Mining), and legacy telecom (Nokia) — cross-sector momentum rotation across the AI infrastructure stack.
items.angle: The cross-sector winners tell you where momentum capital is concentrating: AI compute (CoreWeave), space connectivity (AST), crypto mining (Cipher), and telecom network equipment (Nokia). When Nokia gains 24% in a single week alongside CoreWeave gaining 16%, you are watching the entire AI infrastructure stack — not just the chips — get repriced simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Last week scoreboard: CRWV (CoreWeave) +16%, ASTS (AST SpaceMobile) +16%, NOK +24%, CIFR +19%, CRCL +19%. AI compute, satellite connectivity, crypto mining, legacy telecom all ripping the same week. That is what it looks like when the market decides the entire AI infrastructure stack needs repricing, not just the chip layer.
items.tweetB: Nokia +24% in one week. From 12.4 to 15.4. Same week CoreWeave ran +16%. When the networking equipment layer and the compute layer both rip together, you are not watching a NVDA derivative trade anymore. You are watching the full AI infrastructure stack get bid. Different signal, different duration.
